ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singapore economic rice mixed dish; 'cooked rice dish' (FOODON_00002473) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Singapore economic rice; A00LC (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of economic rice with three vegetable dishes and one fried meat dish, approximately 400 g, as commonly served at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Rice (3 Vegetables 1 Fried Meat) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for 'Economic Rice (3 Vegetables 1 Fried Meat)' (cr_id F05100046). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; value estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried pork and mixed vegetable analogues and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (cooked, Asian-style) as analogous food
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked white rice (Asian-style), which forms the dominant carbohydrate base of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 56.8) / 100 ≈ 40.9, based on 400 g serving with 14.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The input record cited 'Embedded GI Database' at confidence 0.476; this has been replaced with the University of Sydney GI Database as the approved source. GI value of 72 is consistent with published values for cooked white rice.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 266 mg/100 g with low dietary fibre (1.37 g/100 g) and saturated fat from fried meat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g, request reduced sauce, and substitute the fried meat with a steamed or braised protein option to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake; the low fibre content provides insufficient potassium-fibre offset at this sodium level.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI 72) with 14.2 g/100 g carbohydrate and only 1.37 g/100 g dietary fibre producing a high glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should reduce the rice portion to no more than half a cup (approximately 90 g cooked), increase the vegetable serving, replace the fried meat with steamed fish or braised tofu, and consider requesting brown rice where available to lower the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fried meat contributes saturated fat and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); sodium at 266 mg/100 g elevates cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid the fried meat component and substitute with a steamed or grilled lean protein, limit the serving to 300 g or less, and request reduced sauce to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried meat introduces advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace the fried meat with a steamed or braised protein to eliminate AGE formation; the three vegetable dishes are beneficial and should be retained or increased.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic Rice (3 Vegetables, 1 Fried Meat) — known locally as 'cai fan' (菜饭) or 'nasi ekonomi' — is one of Singapore's most ubiquitous hawker and coffee-shop meals. The dish consists of a base of steamed white rice served alongside three self-selected vegetable dishes (commonly stir-fried leafy greens, braised tofu, or egg-based preparations) and one portion of fried meat, typically deep-fried pork, chicken cutlet, or luncheon meat. The combination is assembled to order from a display of pre-cooked dishes, making it a highly variable but nutritionally representative composite. Per 100 g, the dish provides 132 kcal, 4.49 g protein, 6.23 g fat, 14.2 g carbohydrate, 1.37 g dietary fibre, and 266 mg sodium. The white rice base carries a high glycaemic index (GI 72), and the fried meat component contributes saturated fat and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs). Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes (high glycaemic load), hypertension (borderline sodium with low fibre offset), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (AGEs from deep-frying). Nutritional modifications — reducing rice portion, substituting fried meat with steamed protein, and increasing vegetable volume — can substantially improve the health profile. The dish is affordable, widely available, and culturally central to Singapore's multicultural food landscape.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cai fan?
Is nasi ekonomi high in sodium?
What is the GI of economic rice?
Is cai fan suitable for hypertension?
How much protein in economic rice 3 veg 1 fried meat?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100046) at confidence 0.95. GI value of 72 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked white rice. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogues and documented. All mandatory multilingual names present for Singaporean cuisine category.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA SR Legacy data for fried pork and mixed stir-fried vegetables as analogous components; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. The input record listed dietary_tags including 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based', which are incorrect for a dish containing fried meat; these tags have been removed. 4. The input GI source 'Embedded GI Database' at confidence 0.476 has been replaced with the University of Sydney GI Database (cooked white rice analogue, GI 72) as the approved source per Project Veritas source policy. 5. Halal status is marked false as the fried meat component is variable and may include pork; consumers should verify with the specific stall. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (72 × 14.2 g/100 g × 400 g serving) / 100 = 40.9.
